lead_signal.headline: USTR launches 76 new Section 301 trade investigations targeting forced labor and structural overcapacity across dozens of economies, signalling a durable legal architecture for tariff escalation following the February 2026 Supreme Court IEEPA ruling.
lead_signal.confidence_basis: USTR official page confirms 76 new Section 301 investigations launched; PIIE analysis corroborates the legal strategy pivot from IEEPA to Section 301 as a more litigation-resistant tariff authority. Multiple Tier 1 and Tier 2 sources converge on this as the dominant structural trade signal of the current cycle.
lead_signal.note: The Section 301 pivot materially changes the tariff escalation trajectory. Unlike IEEPA-based tariffs struck down in February 2026, Section 301 authority is a statutory trade instrument with a substantially higher bar for judicial invalidation. This is a regime-level shift in the legal durability of US tariff architecture, not merely a tactical manoeuvre. GMM prior assessment treated legal vulnerability as a partial de-escalation pathway; that pathway is now significantly narrowed.

top_developments.title: USTR Launches 76 Section 301 Investigations: Tariff Architecture Shifts to Durable Legal Footing
top_developments.summary: Following the February 2026 Supreme Court ruling that invalidated broad IEEPA-based tariffs, the USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations covering forced labor enforcement failures and structural excess manufacturing capacity across dozens of economies. PIIE analysis notes Section 301 is a statutory trade instrument that is substantially harder to challenge in court than IEEPA, and the Trump administration is simultaneously invoking Section 122 balance-of-payments authority as a 150-day bridge. This represents a structural reconfiguration of the US tariff legal architecture, not a temporary measure, and materially reduces the probability of a court-ordered de-escalation pathway.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Regime-level legal architecture shift: Section 301 authority is a durable statutory basis for tariffs that plays out over months to years; this is not a cyclical or episodic development but a structural recalibration of the US trade policy toolkit following judicial constraint.

sources_cited.excerpt: USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations...60 for inadequate foreign enforcement against forced labor
Presidential tariff actions page updated May 2026 with new Section 301 filings

search_queries_run: US tariff escalation trade war May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: PIIE and USTR sources are Tier 1 and Tier 2 respectively; the legal analysis is corroborated by PIIE's detailed statutory review. High confidence in SA classification given the multi-year enforcement timeline of Section 301 proceedings.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Market pricing as of mid-May 2026 appears to be partially discounting a de-escalation pathway via continued legal challenges. GMM assessment: the Section 301 pivot substantially closes the judicial off-ramp that markets had been pricing as a tail-risk hedge. Divergence is material for EM equities and consumer staples exposed to US import cost pass-through.

top_developments.title: US Supreme Court February 2026 IEEPA Ruling Forces Full Trade Strategy Recalibration
top_developments.summary: The US Supreme Court's February 20, 2026 ruling struck down the broad IEEPA-based tariff framework that had underpinned the Liberation Day (April 2, 2025) tariff package, forcing the Trump administration to invoke Section 122 balance-of-payments authority as a 150-day interim bridge while pursuing Section 301 as a permanent replacement. A coalition of 24 state attorneys general and governors has filed suit to block the BOP tariffs as well. The ruling has created a period of legal uncertainty over the effective tariff rate, with the implied tariff rate from actual payments falling short of announced policy rates.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: a Supreme Court ruling invalidating the primary legal basis for the dominant macro shock of the current cycle is a regime-level event; the legal recalibration plays out over months to years and reshapes the probability distribution of tariff outcomes.

sources_cited.excerpt: The US Supreme Court's February 2026 ruling against many of Trump's 2025 tariffs forced him to recalibrate
A coalition of 24 attorneys general and governors has sued to block the BOP tariffs

search_queries_run: US tariff escalation trade war May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Multiple PIIE sources corroborate the ruling and its consequences. Fed FEDS Notes also reference the gap between announced and actual tariff rates, consistent with legal uncertainty. High confidence.

top_developments.matt_divergence: The gap between statutory and actual tariff rates (confirmed by Fed FEDS Notes) suggests markets may be pricing a higher effective tariff burden than is currently being collected. This creates a downside surprise risk to inflation forecasts if the legal bridge collapses, and an upside surprise if Section 301 tariffs are implemented at full announced rates.

top_developments.title: US-China Trade Decoupling Deepens: Exports to China at Financial Crisis Lows
top_developments.summary: PIIE analysis confirms that US goods exports to China fell to levels not seen since the 2008-09 global financial crisis following China's retaliation against US tariffs, with China effectively halting purchases of US exports in April 2025. Real US imports from China dropped 28% in 2025 alone and are now 40% below pre-first-trade-war levels. The March 2026 Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, and the EU signal a broadening of the decoupling dynamic beyond the bilateral US-China axis.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: a 40% cumulative decline in bilateral trade from 2018 baseline, combined with new multi-country Section 301 investigations, constitutes a regime-level structural decoupling signal, not a cyclical trade fluctuation.

sources_cited.excerpt: US goods shipments to China fell to levels not seen since the global financial crisis of 2008-09
Real US imports from China dropped by 28 percent in 2025 alone

search_queries_run: US tariff escalation trade war May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: PIIE data is drawn from official US trade statistics; the 28% import decline and GFC-level export collapse are quantified findings, not estimates. High confidence in SA classification.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Without Trump's trade wars since 2017, US exports to China would have been nearly 60% higher in 2025 per PIIE modelling. Market pricing of China-exposed US multinationals may not fully reflect the structural permanence of this demand destruction, particularly given the additional Section 301 investigations now underway.

top_developments.title: Federal Reserve Holds Rates at 3.5-3.75%; April 29 FOMC Decision Confirms Stagflation Holding Pattern
top_developments.summary: The FOMC voted unanimously on April 29, 2026 to maintain the federal funds rate target range at 3.5-3.75%, with the interest rate on reserve balances held at 3.65%. Market-based policy rate expectations as of January 2026 indicated only one to two 25bp cuts for the full year, reflecting the Fed's constrained position between tariff-driven inflation and slowing growth. The January FOMC minutes flagged private credit sector vulnerabilities, hedge fund leverage in Treasury markets, and potential spillovers from global bond market volatility as active financial stability concerns.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: the Fed's sustained hold at restrictive rates in the face of tariff-driven stagflation dynamics represents a policy regime constraint, not a cyclical adjustment; the financial stability flags in the January minutes (private credit, hedge fund leverage) are multi-month structural risks.

sources_cited.excerpt: voted unanimously to maintain the interest rate paid on reserve balances at 3.65 percent, effective April 30, 2026
Several participants highlighted vulnerabilities associated with the private credit sector

search_queries_run: Federal Reserve ECB central bank rate decision May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Primary Fed source confirms the April 29 hold decision. January minutes provide the most recent detailed financial stability commentary available. High confidence.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Market pricing of one to two cuts in 2026 appears optimistic relative to GMM's stagflation regime assessment. If tariff-driven inflation proves stickier than the Fed's baseline (consistent with PIIE modelling showing 1pp above-baseline inflation), the cut cycle may be further delayed or cancelled. MATT divergence: market is pricing modest easing; GMM assessment suggests the hold is more durable than priced.

top_developments.title: ECB Holds Rates Unchanged at March 2026 Meeting; Middle East War Drives Stagflationary Revision
top_developments.summary: The ECB Governing Council held all three key rates unchanged at its March 19, 2026 meeting, with the deposit facility rate at 1.93% (€STR). Staff projections revised euro area headline inflation up to 2.6% for 2026 (from December baseline) and cut growth to 0.9% for 2026, both driven by the Middle East war's impact on energy prices. The ECB explicitly flagged that a prolonged energy shock could drive inflation above target through second-round effects while simultaneously depressing growth — a textbook stagflationary configuration for the euro area.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: the ECB's simultaneous upward inflation revision and downward growth revision, driven by an exogenous energy shock, constitutes a stagflationary regime signal for the euro area that plays out over the 2026 projection horizon.

sources_cited.excerpt: headline inflation is seen to average 2.6 per cent in 2026...growth to average 0.9 per cent in 2026
escalation of the Middle East conflict triggered a sharp repricing of near-term policy rate expectations

search_queries_run: Federal Reserve ECB central bank rate decision May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Primary ECB source with staff projections. High confidence. The stagflationary configuration is explicitly described in ECB's own language.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Euro area bank stocks declined 5.2% in the review period per ECB data, while NFC stocks rose 0.8% — a divergence suggesting markets are pricing credit tightening risk into financials but not yet into the broader corporate sector. GMM assessment: if energy prices remain elevated and second-round effects materialise, NFC earnings revisions will follow bank stock repricing with a lag.

top_developments.title: Fed Financial Stability Report (May 2026) Published; Private Credit and Hedge Fund Leverage Flagged
top_developments.summary: The Federal Reserve published its May 2026 Financial Stability Report, with the January FOMC minutes providing the most recent detailed commentary: participants flagged private credit sector vulnerabilities including interconnections with insurance companies and bank exposure, hedge fund leverage and growing footprint in Treasury and equity markets, and the potential for relative value trades to amplify Treasury market shocks. Stablecoin market capitalisation grew approximately 50% in 2025, with the Fed noting stablecoins are increasingly viewed as competitors to traditional transaction accounts. These are active NBFI and systemic risk signals.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Cyclical/Conditional: private credit and hedge fund leverage risks are medium-term structural vulnerabilities that become acute only under specific trigger conditions (liquidity shock, redemption gate, or Treasury market dislocation); absent a trigger, these are conditional rather than immediate systemic risks.

sources_cited.excerpt: Several participants commented on risks associated with hedge funds, including their growing footprint in Treasury and equity markets
stablecoins have grown by about 50 percent in terms of market capitalization...during 2025

search_queries_run: Federal Reserve ECB central bank rate decision May 2026
US Treasury yields bond market May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Primary Fed sources. The May 2026 FSR URL is confirmed as published (federalreserve.gov/publications/files/financial-stability-report-20260508.pdf) but full text not retrieved; January minutes provide the most recent detailed NBFI commentary. Medium-high confidence.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Stablecoin growth of 50% in 2025 is not yet reflected in mainstream financial stability pricing. GMM assessment: the Fed's own FEDS Notes flag stablecoins as potential competitors to transaction accounts — a structural disintermediation risk that is underpriced in bank equity valuations.

top_developments.title: WTO Projects Global Merchandise Trade Volume to Fall 0.2% in 2025; North American Exports Down 12.6%
top_developments.summary: The WTO Secretariat projects global merchandise trade volume to fall 0.2% in 2025, with North American exports forecast to drop 12.6% — the steepest regional decline. Severe downside risks remain, including the potential for a 1.5% global goods trade contraction if reciprocal tariffs are fully applied. The WTO Director-General explicitly cited the US-China standoff and enduring policy uncertainty as the primary brake on global growth, with the most vulnerable least-developed economies facing disproportionate exposure.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: a WTO-projected decline in global merchandise trade volume, driven by structural tariff policy rather than cyclical demand weakness, is a regime-level signal for global trade architecture; the 12.6% North American export decline is a multi-quarter structural disruption.

sources_cited.excerpt: volume of world merchandise trade is likely to fall by 0.2% in 2025...North America exports forecasted to drop by 12.6%
enduring uncertainty threatens to act as a brake on global growth

search_queries_run: US tariff escalation trade war May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Primary WTO source. The April 2025 forecast is the most recent WTO trade volume projection available; the 2026 trajectory will be worse given the Section 301 escalation. High confidence in SA classification.

top_developments.title: PIIE Modelling: Tariffs Reduce US GDP 0.62pp Below Baseline in 2026; Inflation 1pp Above Baseline
top_developments.summary: PIIE's G-Cubed model update (September 2025 baseline) projects US tariffs will reduce the US growth rate by 0.62 percentage points below baseline in 2026 and raise inflation 1 percentage point above baseline, with the price level permanently elevated. Employment losses are concentrated in durable goods manufacturing, mining, and agriculture, with a structural shift toward services at lower real wages. The model finds tariffs alone are insufficient to cause a US recession, but the combination with mass deportation and Fed independence erosion would produce contraction.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: permanent price level elevation and structural employment reallocation are multi-year regime-level effects, not cyclical fluctuations; the PIIE G-Cubed model is a peer-reviewed macroeconomic framework, not anecdotal reporting.

sources_cited.excerpt: tariffs reduce the US growth rate by 0.23 percentage point from baseline in 2025 and by 0.62 percentage point in 2026
US economy is permanently reduced in size every year because the tax wedge from the tariff creates an inefficiency

search_queries_run: US tariff escalation trade war May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: PIIE G-Cubed model is a well-established macroeconomic framework; the September 2025 update incorporates actual tariff rates as of that date. The 2026 projection is directionally conservative given subsequent Section 301 escalation. High confidence.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Equity markets have partially recovered from Liberation Day lows, implying market pricing of a softer growth impact than PIIE's 0.62pp GDP drag. GMM assessment: the permanent price level elevation and structural employment shift are not yet fully priced into consumer staples margins or real estate cap rates.

top_developments.title: Middle East War Drives Energy Price Shock; ECB Flags Stagflationary Scenario; US Gasoline at $6/Gallon in Some Regions
top_developments.summary: PIIE reporting confirms the war in Iran has driven US gasoline prices to as high as $6 per gallon in some US regions, with New England home heating oil rising to $5.58/gallon from $3.60 in December 2025. The ECB's March 2026 projections explicitly incorporate Middle East war scenarios, finding that a prolonged oil and gas supply disruption would push inflation above and growth below baseline. This energy shock is operating simultaneously with the tariff shock, creating a compound stagflationary impulse across both the US and euro area.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural/Analytical: a war-driven energy price shock that has already materially repriced retail energy costs and is incorporated into central bank baseline projections is a regime-level signal; it has been sustained across multiple weeks and is confirmed by two independent Tier 1 and Tier 2 sources (ECB projections + PIIE reporting).

sources_cited.excerpt: war in Iran has driven US gasoline prices to as high as $6 per gallon in some parts of the country
prolonged disruption in the supply of oil and gas would result in inflation being above, and growth being below, the baseline

search_queries_run: Federal Reserve ECB central bank rate decision May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: ECB is Tier 1; PIIE is Tier 2. Two independent sources confirm the energy shock. GMM commodity rule requires 2+ source confirmation for SA classification — satisfied. High confidence.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Energy sector equities may be pricing the oil price spike as a windfall rather than a demand-destruction signal. GMM assessment: at $6/gallon retail gasoline, US consumer discretionary spending compression is a lagged but material risk to consumer staples volumes and real estate transaction activity.

top_developments.title: US-EU and US-Japan Framework Agreements Partially Offset Tariff Escalation; Indonesia and Argentina Deals Signed
top_developments.summary: USTR records confirm a US-EU Framework Agreement on Reciprocal, Fair, and Balanced Trade (August 21, 2025) with tariff-related elements implemented September 25, 2025; a US-Japan Framework Agreement (September 5, 2025); and full agreements with Indonesia (February 19, 2026) and Argentina (February 5, 2026). None of these agreements rolled tariffs back to pre-2025 levels (typically ~3%), and all maintain elevated baseline tariff rates. The framework architecture suggests the US is using tariff threat as a bilateral negotiating lever rather than pursuing multilateral liberalisation, consistent with a Stable-to-Accelerating decoupling direction.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Cyclical/Conditional: bilateral framework agreements are medium-term developments that conditionally moderate (but do not reverse) the structural tariff escalation; their macro impact depends on implementation fidelity and whether Section 301 investigations produce additional tariffs on framework partners.

sources_cited.excerpt: Joint Statement on a United States-European Union Framework on an Agreement on Reciprocal, Fair, and Balanced Trade (August 21, 2025)
none of the agreements or deals rolled tariffs back to their 2024 levels (typically around 3 percent)

search_queries_run: US tariff escalation trade war May 2026
research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: USTR primary source confirms all framework agreements. PIIE confirms tariff levels remain elevated post-agreement. High confidence in CC classification given the conditional nature of implementation.

top_developments.matt_divergence: Markets may be pricing the US-EU and US-Japan frameworks as meaningful de-escalation signals. GMM assessment: PIIE data confirms no rollback to pre-tariff levels; the frameworks are tariff-management instruments, not liberalisation. The de-escalation narrative is overstated relative to the structural tariff floor that remains in place.

tariff_escalation.rung_basis: The tariff regime is at Rung 4: multi-partner retaliation is active (China has been the primary retaliator, with EU retaliatory tariffs of $22bn on US products including bourbon documented, and Canada/Mexico facing sector-specific tariffs), and WTO dispute dynamics are active in the background. The February 2026 Supreme Court IEEPA ruling introduced a domestic legal dimension that partially substitutes for WTO dispute resolution. The March 2026 Section 301 investigations targeting 76 countries across forced labor and overcapacity grounds represent a new escalation vector. No WTO DSB panel has been formally convened as the lead instrument (which would trigger Rung 5), but the multi-partner retaliation and active legal/investigative architecture firmly place the regime at Rung 4.

asset_class_summary.bonds: Bearish/Stagflationary Hold: Fed holds at 3.5-3.75% with only 1-2 cuts priced for full year 2026; longer-term yields face upward pressure from tariff-driven inflation (PIIE: +1pp above baseline) and Middle East energy shock; Treasury curve steepening bias; hedge fund leverage in Treasury futures is an amplification risk per Fed FOMC minutes.
asset_class_summary.tech: Mixed/Cautious: Section 301 investigations targeting Taiwan and China create supply chain disruption risk for semiconductor and hardware supply chains; AI capex cycle continues but faces input cost pressure from tariffs on critical minerals and electronics; no new major AI capex announcements confirmed this week from primary sources.
asset_class_summary.energy: Bullish/Shock-Driven: Middle East war has driven US retail gasoline to $6/gallon in some regions and New England heating oil to $5.58/gallon; ECB baseline incorporates elevated energy prices through 2026; energy sector equities benefit from price spike but demand destruction risk builds at sustained high prices.
asset_class_summary.metals: Bearish/Demand-Constrained: Section 301 investigations targeting structural overcapacity (including China) signal continued tariff pressure on metals imports; US-China decoupling reduces demand for industrial metals in bilateral trade flows; rare earth and critical minerals supply chain vulnerability flagged by PIIE as a persistent structural risk.
asset_class_summary.em_equities: Bearish: US exports to China at GFC lows; Section 301 investigations now targeting Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, EU and dozens of others; WTO projects North American export decline of 12.6%; EM capital flow data not confirmed from EPFR/IIF this cycle (coverage gap — see research_coverage); anecdotal EM stress signals treated as TS only.
asset_class_summary.crypto: Cautious/Structural Watch: Stablecoin market cap grew ~50% in 2025 per Fed FEDS Notes; Fed explicitly flagging stablecoins as potential competitors to transaction accounts and as a financial stability consideration; Bitcoin and broader crypto not directly addressed in primary sources this week; stablecoin regulatory risk is the dominant structural signal.
asset_class_summary.consumer_staples: Bearish/Margin Pressure: Tariff-driven input cost inflation (+1pp above baseline per PIIE) and $6/gallon gasoline compress consumer discretionary spending and logistics costs simultaneously; structural employment shift toward services at lower real wages reduces consumer purchasing power; no earnings guidance data from primary sources this week.
asset_class_summary.real_estate: Bearish: Fed hold at 3.5-3.75% with limited cut path keeps mortgage rates elevated; Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac mortgage portfolio expansion announcement (noted in Jan FOMC minutes) created MBS market volatility; energy cost shock reduces real household income available for housing; commercial real estate faces dual pressure from elevated rates and structural demand shifts.

regime_assessment.conviction_history_note: Stagflation conviction has increased this week. The compound signal of (1) tariff-driven permanent price level elevation confirmed by PIIE modelling, (2) Middle East war energy shock driving $6/gallon gasoline and ECB upward inflation revision to 2.6% for 2026, (3) Fed hold at 3.5-3.75% with constrained cut path, and (4) PIIE GDP drag of 0.62pp below baseline in 2026 — all operating simultaneously — constitutes a high-conviction stagflationary regime. The February 2026 Supreme Court IEEPA ruling introduced a brief de-escalation signal that is now being closed by the Section 301 pivot. Prior week's conviction was Medium-High; this week's evidence upgrades to High.

scenario_probabilities.probability_change_this_week: Base Slow Burn increased from ~50% to 55%: the Section 301 pivot confirms the tariff regime is durable and legally entrenched, reducing the probability of a clean de-escalation pathway. De-escalation decreased from ~25% to 20%: the judicial off-ramp via IEEPA challenges is now substantially closed; bilateral framework agreements do not roll back tariffs to pre-2025 levels. Fast Cascade increased from ~20% to 25%: the Middle East war energy shock (gasoline at $6/gallon, ECB flagging prolonged disruption scenarios) raises the probability of a fast cascade trigger via energy market dislocation rather than the previously modelled Hormuz closure pathway; private credit and hedge fund leverage vulnerabilities flagged in Fed FOMC minutes add a second potential cascade trigger. Probabilities sum to 100.

weekly_brief_narrative:
The macro regime as of the week ending May 19, 2026 is Stagflation, assessed with High conviction. The compound signal structure — tariff-driven permanent price level elevation, Middle East war energy shock, central bank policy paralysis, and structural trade decoupling — has not resolved in any direction this week. It has deepened.

The dominant structural development is the USTR's launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations, covering forced labor enforcement failures and structural overcapacity across dozens of economies including China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, and the EU. This is not a tactical escalation. It is a legal architecture shift. Following the February 20, 2026 Supreme Court ruling that invalidated the IEEPA-based tariff framework underpinning the April 2025 Liberation Day package, the administration has pivoted to Section 301 — a statutory trade instrument with substantially higher resistance to judicial challenge. PIIE analysis is explicit: Section 301 has no readily accessible legal off-ramp. The de-escalation pathway that markets had partially priced via ongoing IEEPA litigation is now materially narrowed. The tariff escalation rung is assessed at T4: multi-partner retaliation active (China, EU, Canada, Mexico), WTO dispute dynamics in background, and a new multi-country investigative front opened under Section 301.

The energy shock is the second structural signal. The war in Iran has driven US retail gasoline to $6/gallon in some regions and New England heating oil to $5.58/gallon. The ECB's March 2026 baseline projections incorporate this shock, revising euro area headline inflation up to 2.6% for 2026 and cutting growth to 0.9% — a textbook stagflationary configuration. The ECB held rates unchanged at its March meeting and is not pre-committing to a rate path. The Fed held at 3.5-3.75% on April 29, with market pricing of only one to two 25bp cuts for the full year. Both major central banks are in a policy holding pattern, constrained by the simultaneous inflation and growth pressures.

Across the eight asset classes: Bonds face bearish pressure from the stagflation configuration and term premium risk from hedge fund leverage in Treasury futures (flagged in Fed FOMC minutes). Tech faces supply chain disruption risk from Section 301 investigations targeting Taiwan and China. Energy is shock-driven bullish but demand destruction risk builds at sustained $6/gallon. Metals face demand compression from US-China decoupling and overcapacity tariff pressure. EM Equities are bearish: US exports to China at GFC lows, Section 301 investigations broadening to EM partners, WTO projecting 12.6% North American export decline. Crypto faces structural regulatory risk from stablecoin growth (50% market cap increase in 2025, per Fed FEDS Notes) and financial stability scrutiny. Consumer Staples face margin compression from tariff input costs and energy-driven logistics inflation. Real Estate remains under pressure from the Fed's constrained cut path and energy cost compression of real household income.

Key MATT divergences: (1) Markets appear to be pricing the US-EU and US-Japan framework agreements as meaningful de-escalation — PIIE data confirms no rollback to pre-tariff levels; the frameworks are tariff-management instruments. (2) Market pricing of 1-2 Fed cuts in 2026 appears optimistic relative to the stagflation configuration; if tariff-driven inflation proves stickier than the Fed's baseline, the cut cycle may be cancelled. (3) Equity markets have partially recovered from Liberation Day lows, implying a softer growth impact than PIIE's 0.62pp GDP drag — this divergence is not yet resolved.

Watch in the coming 7 days: (1) Any Section 301 investigation preliminary findings or tariff rate announcements; (2) Fed speaker circuit at Hoover Institution Monetary Policy Conference (Governors Waller, Bowman, Cook, Barr all scheduled) for forward guidance signals; (3) Middle East energy market developments and any Hormuz-adjacent shipping disruption; (4) WTO dispute filing activity from EU or China in response to Section 301 investigations; (5) Private credit market stress indicators and any redemption gate events in the NBFI sector.

cross_monitor_signals.esa: The US-EU Framework Agreement (August 2025) and its tariff-related implementation (September 2025) have not rolled back tariffs to pre-2025 levels. The ECB's March 2026 decision to hold rates while revising inflation up and growth down reflects the euro area's exposure to both the US tariff shock and the Middle East energy shock simultaneously. The ECB's explicit endorsement of the digital euro and tokenised wholesale central bank money as instruments of European strategic autonomy is a structural ESA signal: Europe is accelerating financial infrastructure independence in response to US policy unpredictability. The February 2026 USTR-EU-Japan joint statement on critical minerals supply chain cooperation is a partial ESA positive but does not offset the tariff floor.
cross_monitor_signals.erm: The Middle East war energy shock ($6/gallon US gasoline, ECB flagging prolonged disruption scenarios) is the dominant ERM signal this week. The ECB explicitly states the energy crisis underscores the imperative to reduce dependence on fossil fuels, accelerating the energy transition policy rationale. Critical minerals supply chain vulnerability — flagged by PIIE as a persistent structural risk, with China having twice nearly halted US automotive production by restricting essential inputs — is the key resource economy signal. The US convened ministerial meetings on critical minerals in early 2026, but PIIE notes real progress requires tariff reductions on allies, which has not occurred.
cross_monitor_signals.scem: US-China trade decoupling is at GFC-level intensity: US exports to China at 2008-09 lows, real US imports from China down 28% in 2025 and 40% below 2018 baseline. China's global imports have declined since 2021 while exports have surged, creating a structural current account imbalance that is a source of global trade tension. The Section 301 investigations targeting structural overcapacity are explicitly directed at China's economic model. The Middle East war energy shock is an active economic warfare vector: PIIE notes the gas and oil price spike is a direct consequence of US military actions, creating a feedback loop between geopolitical conflict and commodity market stress. EM sovereign credit stress is a watch item — EPFR/IIF data not confirmed this cycle.
cross_monitor_signals.agm: No new major AI capex announcements confirmed from primary sources this week. The ECB's March 2026 bulletin notes that earlier in the review period, concerns about overvaluations of AI companies in the US weakened the comovement between US and euro area equity markets, with euro area stocks outperforming. This suggests AI valuation risk is an active cross-asset signal. The Fed FEDS Notes reference India as the world's fastest-growing economy and fifth-largest by current exchange rates — a structural AGM signal for AI infrastructure investment geography. Stablecoin growth (50% market cap in 2025) and the Fed's financial stability scrutiny of DeFi protocols are the dominant AGM-adjacent signals this week.

open_findings.headline: ECB published statement of solidarity with Federal Reserve Chair Powell in January 2026, signed by central bankers worldwide, stressing central bank independence as a cornerstone of stability — a signal of coordinated institutional resistance to political interference in monetary policy.
open_findings.why_it_matters: A coordinated multi-central-bank statement defending Fed independence is an unprecedented institutional signal that political pressure on the Fed is perceived as a systemic risk by global central banking peers; if Fed independence is materially compromised, the fast cascade scenario probability rises sharply.

open_findings.headline: PIIE confirms that without Trump's trade wars since 2017, US exports to China would have been nearly 60% higher in 2025, representing approximately $90 billion in annual foregone export revenue — a permanent structural loss to US agricultural and manufacturing exporters.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The $90bn annual foregone export figure quantifies the cumulative cost of the trade war to US producers and provides a baseline for assessing the fiscal cost of the $11bn farmer subsidy programme announced in December 2025; the structural export loss is not recoverable under current tariff architecture.

open_findings.headline: Fed FEDS Notes confirm that US employment growth slowed notably in the second half of 2025, with the key analytical question being whether weakness stems from labour demand or labour supply — a distinction with material implications for the Fed's dual mandate navigation.
open_findings.why_it_matters: If the employment slowdown is demand-driven (consistent with tariff-induced growth drag), the Fed faces a genuine stagflation dilemma with no clean policy response; if supply-driven (consistent with immigration policy effects), the inflation-employment tradeoff is structurally worse than the Fed's baseline assumes.

open_findings.headline: PIIE notes that Trump's 2026 policy decisions on tariffs and critical minerals have the potential to affect much of the world's economic security, with the easy parts of US-China supply chain decoupling now complete and the hard-to-move parts remaining.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The 'hard-to-move' supply chain segments — semiconductors, rare earths, pharmaceutical inputs, advanced manufacturing — are precisely the sectors where a supply shock would have the highest systemic multiplier; this is the structural foundation of the fast cascade scenario.
